quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd. reported a strong Q1 FY26-27 performance driven by oil prices, with significant contributions from Jio and Retail. The company navigated macroeconomic challenges with agility, maintaining operational resilience and strategic positioning despite geopolitical volatility.

subsections.label: Consolidated performance and what drove it
points: Management framed the quarter as 'extraordinary' due to macro volatility, energy market shock, and supply chain dislocation.
Revenue increased by 25% YoY, driven by oil prices, with Jio and Retail also contributing significantly.
EBITDA exceeded Rs. 54,000 Cr, a 10% increase, while PAT was approximately Rs. 23,200 Cr, a 6% increase.
The consumer businesses now constitute about 50% of the overall mix.
Capex was funded by cash profit, with net debt slightly lower QoQ at approximately Rs. 1.23 lakh Cr.
Moody's upgraded the company's rating to Baa1, and S&P to A-.

subsections.label: Oil-to-Chemicals (Refining & Marketing + Petrochem)
points: O2C revenue increased by 30% YoY, with EBITDA up 17% to approximately Rs. 17,000 Cr.
Management noted that headline cracks/deltas overstate realizable economics due to shock costs.
RIL maintained 96-97% throughput despite a logistics/availability crisis.
Crude diversification and trade/logistics optimization were key mitigation actions.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: JPL revenue was Rs. 39,173 Cr, up 11.8%, with EBITDA at Rs. 20,865 Cr, a 15.1% increase.
Subscribers reached 533m, with 5G users at 285m.
Digital services revenue grew ~20% YoY, outpacing connectivity.
JPL's PAT was Rs. 764 Cr, with finance costs higher due to accounting shifts.

subsections.label: Reliance Retail
points: Q1 revenue was approximately Rs. 90,000 Cr, with underlying growth of 11.6%.
EBITDA was Rs. 6,309 Cr, slightly down YoY, with a margin of 7.9%.
The company is trading near-term margins for digital scale and infrastructure moats.
RCPL revenue was Rs. 8,600 Cr, with rapid scale and supply-chain buildout.

subsections.label: Manufacturing
points: RIL commissioned one of the largest beverage plants in Asia as part of an integrated food park.
The company is exploring an edible oil facility in West Bengal.

subsections.label: Headwinds called out explicitly
points: SAED drag and domestic under-recoveries, including sales to PSUs.
Planned turnaround lowered production meant for sale by 10%.
LPG diversion under government direction impacted petchem feed slate.
Advertising hit by real-money gaming ban and US-Iran war.

subsections.label: Forward view
points: Management maintains confidence in structural positioning despite geopolitical volatility.
Structural support for cracks from lost capacity and Russia's reduced output.
Management refused near-term profitability calls due to renewed SOH volatility.

guidance_ledger.statement: Progress towards achieving 10-gigawatts of integrated solar chain.

risk_register.note: Including sales to PSUs.
Lowered production meant for sale by 10%.
Impacted petchem feed slate.

risk_register.risk: Advertising hit by real-money gaming ban and US-Iran war
risk_register.note: Adverse impact on the overall ad market.
11% depreciation for the year and 4% in March.
Impact on industry and consumer confidence.
Impact on oil supply and prices.
Poses a risk to the business.

risk_register.risk: Natural decline in KG D6 fields
risk_register.note: Efforts to slow decline, productivity improvements in CBM.
Increased OSPs and freight rates due to sanctions.
OPEC unwound 2 million barrels of cuts, China's stock build-up supported prices.
Prices have moved from $11 to $9.5, impacting overall realizations.
Challenges to supply chain security and sufficiency.

themes.label: Geopolitical challenges
themes.note: Management maintains confidence despite geopolitical volatility.

themes.label: Retail expansion
themes.note: RIL is trading margins for digital scale and infrastructure moats.

themes.label: 5G expansion
themes.note: Jio Platforms continues to grow with significant subscriber additions.

themes.label: Diversified growth
themes.note: Revenue growth driven by oil prices, Jio, and Retail contributions.

themes.label: New energy initiatives
themes.note: Structural support for cracks from lost capacity and Russia's reduced output.

key_takeaways: RIL is explicitly trading near-term consumer margins for digital scale (Retail) and building new infrastructure moats (dark stores; data centers; AI/media tooling).

In Energy/O2C, management stresses realizable margin is constrained by procurement/logistics friction even when market cracks are extreme; nonetheless, RIL demonstrated operational resilience (near-full runs) and feedstock advantage (ethane-heavy slate).

Forward commentary is deliberately non-committal on near-term O2C earnings due to geopolitical volatility ('a lot of imponderables'), but management maintains confidence in structural positioning: 'structurally… we are in a good place right through.'

quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd anticipates continued growth across its diverse business segments, with a focus on expanding its 5G network, retail operations, and new energy initiatives, despite geopolitical challenges impacting the oil and gas sector.

key_figures.label: Overall EBITDA growth for the quarter

subsections.label: Group Performance
points: The first 11 months showed robust domestic activity and significant growth in telecom traffic on the 5G network.
Rupee depreciation was a concern, with an 11% drop for the year and 4% in March.
Revenue grew by 10% and EBITDA by 13.5% for FY2025-26, including a one-time gain from the sale of listed shares.
Consumer businesses contributed more than 55% of EBITDA.
JPL and RRVL showed PAT increases of 15% and 12% respectively, with RIL close to 24%.
Digital services showed strong growth with an 18% increase, while retail EBITDA grew by 8%.
O2C grew by 10% despite challenges, and Oil and Gas faced production declines.
Overall EBITDA growth was flat for the quarter, with consumer segments (Jio and retail) up 14%.
Depreciation and interest impacted PAT due to 5G asset capitalization.
Oil-to-Chemicals was down 4%, reflecting a challenging environment with crude supply issues and high logistics costs.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: Jio Platforms ended the year with 524 million subscribers, adding 36.3 million during the year.
The 5G user base reached 268 million, with 77 million added during the year.
Fixed broadband connects reached 27 million, with 10 million added during the year.
JioAirFiber homes reached 12.9 million, with 75% of connections through AirFiber.
Revenue for the year was Rs.1,46,085 Crores, up 14.6% year-on-year.
EBITDA was Rs.76,255 Crores, up 19% year-on-year, with a margin of 52%.
Data traffic increased to 241 Exabytes for the year, a 31% increase year-on-year.

subsections.label: Retail
points: Retail business had the highest ever revenues of Rs.98,000 Crores for the quarter, with 11% growth year-on-year.
EBITDA for the quarter was Rs.6,900 Crores, with a margin of 7.9%.
Hyperlocal commerce showed 30% growth in average daily orders quarter-on-quarter and 300% year-on-year.
The number of registered customers grew by 11% year-on-year, with transactions growing 39%.
333 new stores were opened during the quarter, crossing 20,000 stores in total.
Grocery and fashion segments showed strong double-digit growth.
JioMart serves 1200+ cities with 3100 stores, offering two-hour delivery.

subsections.label: FMCG
points: FMCG business closed with a revenue of Rs.22,000 Crores in 2026, with Q4 revenue at Rs.7350 Crores.
Campa brand delivered Rs.4700 Crores, becoming the fourth largest carbonated soft drink brand in India.
Independence brand delivered Rs.2600 Crores, becoming a promising and trusted brand.
Package drinking water business became the third largest in India.
International presence expanded to 40 countries.

subsections.label: JioStar
points: JioStar achieved a monthly active user reach of 550 million in March, driven by sports and entertainment.
Broke a world record for concurrent streams at 72.5 million during the T20 World Cup.
Launched AI-driven technologies like Tadka, ChatGPT integration, and in-app commerce with Swiggy.
Sports properties like the Women's Premier League and T20 World Cup drove engagement.
Entertainment content like Bigg Boss and Chiraiya showed strong viewership growth.

subsections.label: Retail Expansion
points: Opened 333 new stores during the quarter, crossing the milestone of 20,000 stores.
Grocery segment crossed 1000 big box hypermarket supermarkets, the fastest rollout globally.

subsections.label: FMCG Manufacturing
points: Investing in manufacturing capabilities to become one of the largest manufacturers of cooldrinks in India.
Expanding with 12 plants across India and setting up integrated food parks for cost efficiencies.

subsections.label: Geopolitical Challenges
points: Rupee depreciation of 11% for the year and 4% in March is a concern.
Supply shock and its impact on industry and consumer confidence are major concerns.
Middle East conflict and its impact on oil supply and prices are significant challenges.
SAED reintroduction poses a risk to the business.

subsections.label: Outlook
points: Continued focus on expanding 5G network, retail operations, and new energy initiatives.
Geopolitical challenges in the oil and gas sector remain a concern.

guidance_ledger.statement: Progress towards achieving 10-gigawatts of integrated solar chain.

risk_register.note: 11% depreciation for the year and 4% in March.
Impact on industry and consumer confidence.
Impact on oil supply and prices.
Poses a risk to the business.

risk_register.risk: Natural decline in KG D6 fields
risk_register.note: Efforts to slow decline, productivity improvements in CBM.
Increased OSPs and freight rates due to sanctions.
OPEC unwound 2 million barrels of cuts, China's stock build-up supported prices.
Prices have moved from $11 to $9.5, impacting overall realizations.
Challenges to supply chain security and sufficiency.

themes.label: 5G expansion
themes.note: Significant growth in 5G user base and network expansion.

themes.label: Retail expansion
themes.note: Strong growth in retail revenue and store expansion.

themes.label: New energy initiatives
themes.note: Progress in green energy projects and new contracts.

themes.label: Geopolitical challenges
themes.note: Middle East conflict impacting oil supply and prices.

themes.label: Diversified growth
themes.note: Continued growth across diverse business segments.

key_takeaways: The first 11 months seem pretty different from what happened in March and in 11 months it seemed like fastest growing economy, domestic activity was fairly robust, and from our own telecom point of view significant growth in traffic on the 5G network and so on.

All the measures taken on GST rationalization, the easing rate cycle, monsoons were good and that gave broadly consumption tailwinds were decent there, energy prices also give or take, was range bound and even growth in both fuel and refined products remained steady and then you go into March.

We all know the numbers almost doubling of 70% higher in some cases, even in gas it went up to double, of course the concern for everybody and all of us is the fact that the supply shock and its impact on industry and consumer confidence, that is something that as it is happening, everybody is grappling with it.

Rupee depreciation obviously is an area of concern, 11% for the year and 4% just in March and the bias will be there if this situation were to persist because on the back of widening gap, these are concern areas, it all depends on what is the outcome of the war, when does the settlement happen, but were it not these are really the immediate concern areas in everybody’s mind.

In this context, when I just look at FY2025-26 the full year and before I go into the quarter 10% up on revenue, 13.5% on EBITDA, this of course includes the one time that we had in the sale of listed shares.
Now, with the consumer businesses contributing more than 55% of EBITDA and PAT growth was also good, and I have provided the details of the standalone profitability of RIL, JPL and RRVL.
So, JPL at year-on-year PAT increase is about 15% and RRVL about 12%, RIL close to 24 and this is really the mix.
Overall growth 13%, digital very strong 18%, subscribers, 5G subscriptions, broadband mix, customer engagement, all these metrics are very good strong numbers.
Retail 8% growth on EBITDA and this is muted because of the scale up of the hyperlocal e-commerce.
Also, fashion and lifestyle demand was a bit soft.

O2C up 10% despite everything and as Srini will talk through in the presentations about what happened there and Oil and Gas has been more about the fall in overall production, the reservoir reserves coming down.
This I just thought, just put a five- year context.
Overall is up more than 2x, but digital is again more than 2x, retail two and a half times, O2C close to two.
So, in a more broader timeframe I just wanted to emphasize this more than doubling is something that we have been doing and this is again another indication.
Just going into for the quarter, as you can see overall EBITDA growth is flat and actually it is about consumer.

When I say consumer here I am taking both Jio and retail that is up 14%, which really negates the impact of energy, which has been lower and more on PAT has been on a quarter-on-quarter lower because of depreciation and interest on the back of the capitalization of the 5G assets, and more specifically on Oil-to-Chemicals down 4% and actually the 4% does not bring the overall context of how difficult the environment was.
One, of course, last year, year back, same time the overall numbers were strong and when you

look at what happened in terms of just physical inability to get crude, in fact if you see the throughput for us it was lower by 4% and just getting physical crude, the premium which was trading on top of the benchmarks, logistics cost, insurance cost, the sheer volatility that we saw there and coupled with under recoveries on fuel retailing, the introduction of SAED, there are million things which have happened in this quarter and that is what I meant by saying that 4% number really does not capture what the environment was and in that sense
I am very happy or delighted to look at this performance and the quality of the performance is very strong.

Oil and Gas because of gas volumes, digital services has been good, 16% on the back of subscriber additions, which was up 7.5%, and the 5G user base has been growing at about 40% and retail more broad based consumption that we have seen and the hyperscale expansion, which Dinesh will talk through in the retail presentations and this is again, same five year perspective I just thought to put that overall still net debt we have been able to keep that in control or continuing the capex vis a vis what is the cash profit, all of those trends have remained fairly strong and these numbers say that the EBITDA has been at about 0.64, which against what we have been talking about below one.
pressing_questions: Now, my understanding is if alternate crudes continue to be sort of the only option that we have, the dispute sustains for a while longer, now the chemical composition of crudes from the US and Africa and even Venezuela are markedly different in terms of sulfur content, API and others. So, how much of distillate yield can actually change because obviously, US crudes and Venezuelan crudes are geared towards more of light distillates. I think Russian and Middle East crudes are obviously something that are more optimal from our Indian distillate yield perspective, so do you see that as a risk at all? If, those are the only crudes that are available for us?

The second question that I had, Sir, was with respect to the fuel retail, where I think it was mentioned very clearly that we have unlike earlier occasions when margins were turned negative, operations would be curtailed. We have managed to continue our operations. But is there some sort of a level at which we sort of look at then, sort of price increases, because obviously losses are significant even for us, I would presume, in this quarter. So how are we looking at that business in terms of?

My question is again on the refining side only. So, as you had indicated that in the month of March, in the Q4, we did see some volatility in terms of procurement, freight, and other factors. So how is the situation now in the Q1? How comfortable we are in terms of managing our procurement, freight, and other costs? And how should we see the margin environment panning out for us, particularly on both refining and petrochemical side for the rest of the year?
What percentage of our production is impacted by SAED?

One question on telecom. Anshuman, in your statement, you talked about differentiated services through network slicing. What are we looking there? And what is the opportunity in terms of increasing the realization or increasing the subscriber? How are we looking there?
We are not looking at anything on B2C kind of a business in the mobility?
In the voice or data, we are not looking to do?

There seems to be a fair bit of competition in the market. Horizontal e-commerce players seem to be adding a fair bit of dark stores. And of course, you have the three quick commerce players. In that scenario, how do you see the industry consolidating in the next few years and what are your ambitions about the quick commerce industry? Do you, like a dark store target, a market share target, a user target and would you be like a consolidator in the industry if the opportunity comes? That would be my first question.

quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd continues to show strong growth across its diversified businesses, with significant progress in new energy initiatives and 5G expansion, despite global uncertainties.

subsections.label: Group Performance
points: Customer addition strong at about 9 million, total 515 million customers.
Retail revenue growth 8%, EBITDA growth 1.3%, store count close to 20,000.
Quick commerce run rate at 1.6 million orders, on track to be second largest QC player.
FMCG turnover more than 5,000 Crores, 60% up year-on-year.
Energy business growth 15%, transportation fuel deltas strong, 60% to 100% growth year-on-year.
Jio-bp volumes growth 24% for gas oil and gasoline.
EBITDA lower on E&P due to lower volumes and price.
Revenue up 10%, EBITDA up 6%, PAT at 22,290 Crores, up 1.6%.
Digital services revenue up 16%, EBITDA driven by strong O2C performance, 15% higher.
Consol profit muted due to higher finance and depreciation, primarily from Jio 5G assets.

subsections.label: Retail
points: Highest ever revenue at 97,600 Crores, up 8.1%.
Factors impacting margin: festive offers, hyperlocal commerce investments, new labour code.
Hyperlocal commerce growth: 1.6 million orders, 53% QoQ growth, 360% YoY growth.
Exclusive partnership with Fabletics, American women's athleisure brand.
Grocery big box key driver of growth, strong LFL growth.
JioMart largest quick commerce footprint, 5,000+ pin codes, 1,000+ cities.
Ajio digital fashion business, average value up 21% YoY.
Shein app installs crossed 6.5 million, portfolio 50,000+ options.
Jewels business, average bill values up 73%, best ever Dhanteras with 21% LFL growth.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: 515 million subscribers, 8.9 million net adds.
5G user base 253 million+, 65% share of 5G subscribers in India.
Revenues crossed 37,000 Crores, EBITDA margin 52%.
5G deployment helping win more customers, 53% of network traffic on 5G.
Partnerships like JioHotstar and Google Gemini Pro enhancing offerings.
Enterprise side offering managed services beyond connectivity.

subsections.label: JioStar
points: Platform growth in entertainment and sports content.
Bigg Boss franchise posted highest ever ad revenues, 40% growth in digital watch time.
Women's World Cup live watch time 10x over previous, final match viewership on par with men's cricket.
Kabaddi viewership doubled, second biggest sport after cricket.
450 million average monthly active users, 13% growth over previous quarter.
Record high monetization on digital entertainment.

subsections.label: O2C - Refining & Marketing
points: Fuel cracks up 60% to 100%, strong domestic market placement through Jio-bp.
Diesel sales up 25%, gasoline MS sales up 21%.
EBITDA 16,507 Crores, 15% growth.
Challenges: higher feedstock costs, low petrochemical deltas, high freight rates.
Crude throughput 20.6, maximizing throughput in strong market environment.
Domestic market focus, innovative products, fleet and driver programs.

subsections.label: O2C - Petrochemicals
points: Ethane prices up 21%, naphtha prices down 14%.
Ethylene capacity increase, operating rates down to 80%.
Domestic market focus, inventory management, sales discipline.
Industry restructuring in Far East Asia, capacity rationalization expected.
Domestic demand: polyethylene and polypropylene growth, PVC demand reduction due to prolonged monsoon.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms Technology
points: Proprietary technologies developed for Indian customers, suitable for global scale.
4G and 5G core run on in-house stack, world's largest fixed wireless operator.
Home OS and tele OS scaled to over 25 million homes.
Innovations in devices and OS, applications developed and launched.
99% population reach, million plus touch points for scaling technologies.

subsections.label: New Energy Manufacturing
points: Fully integrated 10-gigawatt peak annual solar manufacturing gigafactory on track.
Solar module manufacturing expanded, high yield of 94%-95%.
Solar cell manufacturing based on heterojunction technology, ramping up to full capacity.
Pilot facility for ingot and wafer, expanding to giga scale.
Polysilicon and glass facilities world-class, commissioning during current year.
Battery manufacturing progressing, 40-gigawatt hour capacity, expanding to 100-gigawatt hour.
Kutch site development for solar and battery installation, significant progress.

subsections.label: FMCG Strategic Moves
points: RCPL became direct subsidiary of RIL from 1st December 2025.
Acquisition of Udhaiyam, expanding pan-India staples business.
Acquisition of global brands like Brylcreem, Toni and Guy, Badedas, and Matey.
Entry into pet categories, high-quality, science-backed products.
Relaunch of SIL, entry into noodle segment.

subsections.label: Oil & Gas Exploration & Production
points: Natural decline in KG D6 fields, efforts to slow decline.
LNG prices moved from $11 to $9.5, impacting realizations.
CBM productivity improved, better performance in recent campaign.
Gas prices stable, Indian demand robust, PNGRB uniform tariff policy positive.

subsections.label: O2C - Refining & Marketing
points: Higher feedstock costs due to increased OSPs and freight rates.
Sanctions on vessels reduced VLCC availability, increasing freight costs.
Oversupply in market, OPEC unwound 2 million barrels of cuts.
China's stock build-up supported prices, refinery operating rates high.

subsections.label: New Energy Outlook
points: Progress on integrated solar and battery manufacturing, commissioning during current year.
Kutch solar power generation to deliver significant capacity annually.
Expansion plans for solar and battery manufacturing to 20-gigawatt peak and 100-gigawatt hour respectively.

guidance_ledger.statement: Progress towards achieving 10-gigawatts of integrated solar chain.

risk_register.risk: Natural decline in KG D6 fields
risk_register.note: Efforts to slow decline, productivity improvements in CBM.
Increased OSPs and freight rates due to sanctions.
OPEC unwound 2 million barrels of cuts, China's stock build-up supported prices.
Prices have moved from $11 to $9.5, impacting overall realizations.
Challenges to supply chain security and sufficiency.

themes.label: Diversified growth
themes.note: Strong growth across diversified businesses, despite global uncertainties.

themes.label: New energy initiatives
themes.note: Significant progress in integrated solar and battery manufacturing.

themes.label: 5G expansion
themes.note: Continued growth in 5G subscribers and network traffic.

themes.label: Retail expansion
themes.note: Retail business shows strong revenue growth and strategic partnerships.

themes.label: Geopolitical challenges
themes.note: Impact on feedstock costs and freight rates due to sanctions and oversupply.

key_takeaways: Customer addition strong at about 9 million, taking the total to about 515 million customers.
Retail revenue growth has been about 8%, and overall EBITDA growth has been 1.3%.
Quick commerce is progressing with a run rate of 1.6 million orders, on track to be the second largest QC player.
FMCG turnover more than 5,000 Crores, 60% up on a year-on-year basis.
Energy business side, strong growth, 15% and transportation fuel deltas strong, 60% to 100% growth year on year.
Jio-bp continues to do well with 24% growth in volumes for both gas oil and gasoline.
Revenue is up 10%, EBITDA up about 6%, and overall, PAT at 22,290 crores, which was also up about 1.6%.
Digital service up 16%.
Consol profit was muted because of higher finance and depreciation, Jio depreciation with capitalization of 5G assets was the primary driver for higher depreciation.

quarters.summary: Infosys anticipates a challenging FY27 with a revenue growth guidance of 1.5%-3.5% CC YoY, factoring in macroeconomic headwinds and strategic decisions to not pursue certain deals. The company remains focused on AI initiatives and large deal wins to drive growth.

subsections.label: Margins
points: FY26 operating margin was 21%; Q4 was 20.9%.
Gains from currency and Project Maximus were reinvested in talent, AI, and sales and marketing.

subsections.label: Cash & Working Capital
points: DSO was 78, the lowest in 7 years.
FY26 FCF was $3.5bn (adjusted), with FCF conversion at 106% for the year and 111% for Q4.

subsections.label: Capital Return
points: Returned over $4bn to shareholders in FY26.
Proposed a final dividend of INR 25, totaling INR 48, which is a 11.6% YoY increase.

subsections.label: Large Deals
points: FY26 TCV for large deals was $14.9–$15.0bn, a 28% YoY increase, with 55% net new.
Q4 TCV was $3.2bn across 19 large deals.
Management emphasized that the large deal pipeline continues to remain strong.

subsections.label: Vertical/Geography Mix
points: Outperformance in Financial Services, Communications, Manufacturing, and Europe.
Communications, Manufacturing, and Europe grew more than double the company average, led by large deal wins.

subsections.label: Financial Services
points: FS grew 4.4% in FY26, driven by large-deal ramps, AI-led transformation, legacy modernization, and vendor consolidation.
Market sentiment remains positive with continued consumer spending across US banking, capital markets, and Europe.
CY26 budgets are expected to grow in the US.
Infosys is a strategic AI partner for 18 out of the top 20 clients.
Notable deals include a large GCC deal for a regional bank in the US and a large AI-first GCC deal.

subsections.label: EURS (Energy/Utilities/Resources/Services)
points: Demand is constructive, supported by a strong large deal pipeline and vendor consolidation driven by cost takeout.
Energy sector sees increased outsourcing leading to healthy deal momentum.
Utilities face structurally higher demand due to grid constraints, renewables integration, and acceleration of electricity needs for data centers.
80% of the large deal TCV of FY26 was net new, positioned as a driver of FY27 acceleration.

subsections.label: Manufacturing
points: Cautious amid softer demand, particularly in automotive and parts of Europe, with tariffs and ongoing Middle East conflict causing delayed decisions.
Focus on cost optimization and operational resilience.
Near term and FY27 growth will be impacted due to low revenue from one large client.

subsections.label: Retail & Communications
points: Retail budgets are tightly controlled, with discretionary spending under pressure; clients want AI productivity to do more with similar budgets.
Communications budgets are flat to negative, with discretionary spending impacted; shift from Generative to Agentic AI, with clients consolidating IT+BPM to cut costs.
Uptick in AI deals in IT operations, software replacement, and mainframe migration.

subsections.label: AI Strategy
points: Infosys reiterated its AI thesis from Investor Day, highlighting a large addressable market for AI services across six areas: AI strategy & engineering, data, process, legacy modernization, physical AI, and trust.
Core platforms Topaz Fabric (AI) and Cobalt (cloud) are operational today.
Management pushed back on 'pilot-only' narratives, citing production-scale examples.

subsections.label: Proof Points
points: Ralph Lauren: conversational/personalized AI tool increased revenue by 12% and customer engagement by 50%.
Hertz: migrated 3 million lines of COBOL code to microservices using foundation models, reducing cost by 60% and timeline by 60%.
BP: 50 AI agent initiatives across multiple functions, achieving 95% payment accuracy, 50% faster contract validation, and 18% improvement in IT operations efficiency.

subsections.label: Partner Ecosystem and Internal Rollout
points: New partnerships with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, NVIDIA, Microsoft, AWS, Google Cloud, and Intel.
30,000+ developers on GitHub Copilot.

90%+ of employees have been skilled on different types of AI platforms; fresher program includes tiered compensation for AI-aligned skills and a 'forward deployed engineer' construct for client-facing AI build work.

subsections.label: AI Revenue Disclosure
points: AI contribution is above the prior disclosed 5.5%, but management withheld updated percentages.
Management noted deliberate disclosure tightening, stating they shared 5.5% at Investor Day to show materiality and growth.

subsections.label: AI Spend vs. New Spend
points: Management described a two-sided ledger: compression in some services due to efficient foundation models/tooling, and growth in other services.
Productivity pass-through is acknowledged as a margin headwind, with competitive intensity leading to productivity being passed back to clients.
Growth engine (AI-first work, net new large deals, footprint expansion) currently exceeds compression.

subsections.label: Acquisitions/JV in Guidance
points: Stratus (insurance-related) is included in guidance, contributing ~25 bps to FY27 growth.
Optimum Healthcare and Versent JV are excluded as they are pending regulatory approvals.

subsections.label: Geopolitics
points: Management acknowledged a change in the economic environment after Middle East/Iran conflict escalation but sees paths towards stabilization.
Macro stability is tied to tech spend recovery.

subsections.label: Competitive Intensity & 'Irrational' Bids
points: Management acknowledged occasional competitor proposals outside the range of what models can deliver; Infosys may walk away, as done in the European manufacturing case for ROIC discipline.

subsections.label: AI Security/Vulnerability Angle (Mythos)
points: CEO described Mythos as exposing more vulnerabilities than expected, framing it as a services opportunity to help clients protect vulnerabilities.

subsections.label: Leadership/Succession Questions
points: CEO gave 'No comment' on tenure/succession-related queries.

subsections.label: FY27 Outlook
points: Revenue guidance is 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY, with the lower end assuming higher deterioration in the environment and the upper end assuming an improved environment.
Operating margin guidance is 20%–22%.
Guidance includes contribution from Stratus but excludes Optimum Healthcare and Versent JV.
Known headwind baked into FY27 guidance: 0.75%–1% revenue reduction due to lower revenue from a large European manufacturing client.
Additional guidance drags from delivery mix: expected onsite mix reduction of 0.75%–1%; third-party costs expected to be similar to FY26 levels.
Guidance range is narrower than last year due to better clarity on the environment and client behavior.

guidance_ledger.statement: Revenue guidance is 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY, with the lower end assuming higher deterioration in the environment and the upper end assuming an improved environment.
0.75%–1% revenue reduction due to lower revenue from a large European manufacturing client.

risk_register.note: Management acknowledged a change in economic environment after conflict escalation, but sees paths towards stabilization.
Management acknowledged occasional competitor proposals outside the range of what models can deliver; Infosys may walk away.
CEO described Mythos as exposing more vulnerabilities than expected, framing it as a services opportunity.
CEO gave 'No comment' on tenure/succession-related queries.
Security breaches are increasing, with state and non-state actors using AI for attacks.
Legacy systems pose security risks and hinder innovation due to data silos.
The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS, and free cash flow for the quarter.
Infosys is monitoring the U.S. government's plans to cap credit card interest limits and its potential impact on clients.
Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.

themes.label: AI Initiatives
themes.note: Infosys reiterated its AI thesis and highlighted production-scale examples and partnerships.

themes.label: Legacy Modernization
themes.note: AI-led transformation and legacy modernization are key growth drivers in Financial Services.

themes.label: Strategic Partnerships
themes.note: New partnerships with OpenAI, Anthropic, and others enhance AI capabilities.

themes.label: Security Challenges
themes.note: Mythos exposes vulnerabilities, presenting a services opportunity for Infosys.

themes.label: AI Services Growth
themes.note: AI services contribution is growing, with management confirming it exceeds 5.5%.

key_takeaways: FY26 revenue growth was 3.1% in constant currency, with Q4 growth at 4.1% YoY and a decline of 1.3% QoQ due to seasonality and slower decision making.
Large deals TCV for FY26 was $14.9–$15.0bn, a 28% YoY increase, with 55% net new.
Operating margin for FY26 was 21%, with Q4 at 20.9%.
Infosys returned over $4bn to shareholders in FY26 and proposed a final dividend of INR 25, totaling INR 48, a 11.6% YoY increase.
FY27 revenue guidance is 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY, with operating margin guidance at 20%–22%.
AI contribution is above the prior disclosed 5.5%, but management withheld updated percentages.
Management described a two-sided ledger for AI, with compression in some services and growth in others.

quarters.summary: Infosys is focusing on AI-driven transformation across its services, emphasizing modernization, talent transformation, and strategic partnerships to harness AI's potential and address deployment gaps.

subsections.label: AI Transition
points: AI transition is faster than previous tech shifts, reaching 1 billion users in a couple of years compared to the Internet's 10 years and smartphones' 5 years.
AI is a fundamental change in business operations, requiring AI-native architecture and changes in customer journeys.
Modernization of legacy systems is crucial to leverage AI, as many companies spend 60% to 80% of IT budgets on maintenance.
AI's speed is facilitated by existing Internet and smartphone infrastructure.
AI requires a shift in talent focus from coding to AI orchestration and implementation.

subsections.label: AI Strategy and Engineering
points: Infosys is engaged in AI strategy work, building agents, and orchestrating platforms.
AI strategy involves creating an AI blueprint and transformation office, focusing on unlocking AI value in specific business units.
AI strategy includes setting up innovation labs and identifying core processes for AI integration.

subsections.label: AI Augmented Services
points: Infosys is infusing AI into traditional services like application development, testing, and business operations.
Partnerships with leading AI models and tools, such as Anthropic and OpenAI, are integral to service delivery.
Infosys has trained 90% of its developers on AI, emphasizing the need for great engineering talent.

subsections.label: AI-first Services
points: Infosys has developed a playbook for AI-first services, focusing on AI strategy, data management, process reimagination, and modernization.
AI-first services include AI strategy and engineering, data for AI, process reimagination, modernization, physical AI, and AI trust.
Infosys is working with clients like Danske Bank and Nova Chemicals to implement AI strategies and solutions.

subsections.label: Strategic Partnerships
points: Infosys has a joint venture with Telstra for B2B non-connectivity solutions in Australia.
Partnerships with AI-native partners are crucial for leveraging external innovation and accelerating AI adoption.
Infosys is collaborating with universities for joint research on agentic technology and AI scaling.

subsections.label: Security and Modernization Challenges
points: Security breaches are increasing, with state and non-state actors using AI for attacks.
Legacy systems pose security risks and hinder innovation due to data silos.
Modernization is necessary to address security and operational challenges.

subsections.label: AI Services Growth
points: AI services represent 5.5% of Infosys' revenue in Q3, growing at a robust pace.
The AI services market opportunity is estimated between $300 billion and $400 billion by 2030.

guidance_ledger.statement: AI services represent 5.5% of our revenue in Q3, and it is growing at a robust pace.
Our operating margin guidance for the financial year remains the same at 20% to 22%.

risk_register.note: Security breaches are increasing, with state and non-state actors using AI for attacks.
Legacy systems pose security risks and hinder innovation due to data silos.
The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS, and free cash flow for the quarter.
Infosys is monitoring the U.S. government's plans to cap credit card interest limits and its potential impact on clients.
Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.

themes.label: AI Initiatives
themes.note: Infosys is focusing on AI-driven transformation across its services.

themes.label: Legacy Modernization
themes.note: Modernization of legacy systems is crucial to leverage AI and address security risks.

themes.label: Strategic Partnerships
themes.note: Partnerships with AI-native partners and universities are crucial for innovation and AI adoption.

themes.label: Security Challenges
themes.note: Security breaches are increasing, necessitating modernization and AI integration.

themes.label: AI Services Growth
themes.note: AI services represent 5.5% of revenue in Q3, with significant growth potential.

key_takeaways: AI transition is faster than previous tech shifts, reaching 1 billion users in a couple of years compared to the Internet's 10 years and smartphones' 5 years.
AI is a fundamental change in business operations, requiring AI-native architecture and changes in customer journeys.
Modernization of legacy systems is crucial to leverage AI, as many companies spend 60% to 80% of IT budgets on maintenance.
AI's speed is facilitated by existing Internet and smartphone infrastructure.
AI requires a shift in talent focus from coding to AI orchestration and implementation.

quarters.summary: Infosys has revised its revenue growth guidance upwards to 3% to 3.5% for FY26, driven by strong large deal wins and AI adoption, particularly in Financial Services and Energy sectors.

subsections.label: Margin Analysis
points: Adjusted operating margins increased by 20 basis points sequentially to 21.2%.
Operating margins including the impact of change in labor codes stands at 18.4%.
The impact of higher variable pay was partly offset by one-off benefits during the quarter.

subsections.label: Cash Flow and Capital Allocation
points: Free cash flow generation adjusted for labor codes remained robust at $965 mn, which is 113% of adjusted net profit.
During the quarter, Infosys completed its largest ever buyback, returning INR18,000 crores to shareholders.

key_figures.label: Total Large Deal TCV for 9 months
Net New Deal TCV for 9 months

subsections.label: Large Deals
points: Large deal TCV was robust at $4.8 bn in Q3 with 57% net new.
Total large deal TCV for 9 months stood at $11.7 bn, exceeding the total large deal TCV of full year FY'25.
Net new deal TCV for 9 months was up by 40% as compared to the same period last year.
Large deal pipeline continues to be healthy.

subsections.label: Vertical Performance
points: Momentum in Financial Services continues with 3.9% year-on-year growth in constant currency terms.
Among geos, Europe continued to lead the growth by 7.2% year-on-year in constant currency terms.
Volumes continue to remain soft for the quarter and the year.
On-site mix further reduced by 10 basis points in Q3 and by 70 basis points in 9 months FY'26.

subsections.label: Talent and Utilization
points: Utilization, excluding trainees was down by 1% sequentially at 84.1% and including trainees, was down by 2.2% to 80% as Infosys continues to create capacity for future growth opportunities.
Net headcount increased by 5,000 to 337,000 employees.
LTM attrition declined by 2% sequentially and 1.4% on a year-on-year basis, reflecting both market conditions and Infosys' focus on employee retention and upskilling.

key_figures.label: Lines of Code Generated Using AI

subsections.label: AI Initiatives
points: Infosys has deepened its Topaz AI capability with an agent services suite called Topaz Fabric.
Infosys is expanding strategic partnerships with AI companies, including a partnership with Cognition.
Infosys is recognized as a leader in AI across 12 ratings in FY26.
Infosys is working with 90% of its 200 largest clients to unlock value with AI.
Infosys is currently working on 4,600 AI projects, generating over 28 mn lines of code using AI and has built over 500 agents.

subsections.label: Customer Engagement and AI Adoption
points: Infosys is a preferred AI partner for top 15 out of 25 banking clients.
Infosys is witnessing six AI-led value pools emerging that could unlock a large incremental opportunity.
Infosys aspires to make AI work for clients delivering business outcomes for cost, revenue growth, and innovation.

subsections.label: M&A Strategy
points: Infosys has done acquisitions in cyber, consulting, and energy services and will continue with that approach.
Infosys has a good pipeline of possible companies for acquisition and discussions are ongoing.

subsections.label: Labor Code Impact
points: The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS, and free cash flow for the quarter.
The ongoing impact of the labor code will be approximately 15 basis points on an annual basis.

subsections.label: Geopolitical and Regulatory Challenges
points: Infosys is monitoring the U.S. government's plans to cap credit card interest limits and its potential impact on clients.
Infosys has stated that no employee has been apprehended by U.S. authorities, addressing concerns about H-1B visa issues.

guidance_ledger.statement: The new revenue growth guidance for this financial year is 3% to 3.5% growth in constant currency.
Our operating margin guidance for the financial year remains the same at 20% to 22%.

risk_register.note: The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS, and free cash flow for the quarter.
Infosys is monitoring the U.S. government's plans to cap credit card interest limits and its potential impact on clients.
Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.

themes.label: AI Initiatives
themes.note: Infosys is expanding its AI capabilities and partnerships, working on 4,600 AI projects.

themes.label: Large Deals
themes.note: Infosys reported a strong large deal TCV of $4.8 billion in Q3, with 57% net new deals.

themes.label: Revenue Growth Guidance
themes.note: Infosys has revised its revenue growth guidance for FY26 upwards to 3% to 3.5%.

themes.label: Visa and Regulatory Challenges
themes.note: Infosys is monitoring U.S. regulatory changes and addressing H-1B visa concerns.

themes.label: Regional Performance
themes.note: Europe led growth with a 7.2% year-on-year increase in constant currency terms.

key_takeaways: Infosys has revised its revenue growth guidance for FY26 upwards to 3% to 3.5% in constant currency terms.
The company reported a strong large deal TCV of $4.8 billion in Q3, with 57% net new deals.
Infosys is expanding its AI capabilities and partnerships, notably with Cognition, and is working on 4,600 AI projects.
The adjusted operating margin for Q3 was 21.2%, with a free cash flow of $915 million.
Infosys completed its largest ever buyback, returning INR18,000 crores to shareholders.

quarters.summary: Infosys reported strong Q2 FY26 performance with revenue growth and increased market share, driven by large deals and AI initiatives. The company maintained its operating margin guidance and raised its revenue growth guidance for the full year.

subsections.label: Quarterly Performance
points: Revenues for the quarter grew 2.2% sequentially and 2.9% year-on-year in constant currency terms.
Operating margin was 21%.
Free cash flow was $1.1 billion, which is 131% of the net profit.
EPS in rupee terms grew by 13% year-on-year to Rs. 17.6.

subsections.label: Cash Flow and Investments
points: Consolidated cash and investments were at $6.2 billion at the end of the quarter.
Yield on cash balance was at 6.98% and ROE stood at 29.1%.

subsections.label: Guidance and Outlook
points: Revenue growth guidance for the financial year is 2% to 3% in constant currency terms.
Operating margin guidance remains at 20% to 22% for the full year.

key_figures.label: Net New Work in Large Deals
Net New in H1 Deal Wins

subsections.label: Deal Activity
points: Large deals were at $3.1 billion with 67% net new.
A mega deal worth $1.6 billion was announced after the close of the quarter.
H1 deal wins were at $6.9 billion with net new at 60% plus.

subsections.label: Industry Performance
points: Financial Services and Manufacturing grew above 5% year-on-year in constant currency.
Europe also grew greater than 5% year-on-year in constant currency terms.

subsections.label: AI Initiatives
points: Infosys is delivering more than 2,500 Generative AI and AI projects and 200-plus agentic AI projects for clients.
Infosys has built capabilities in AI, applied them across operations, and delivers these innovations to clients through Infosys Topaz.

subsections.label: Regional Performance
points: Europe has done extremely well with good investments in different countries.
The U.S. market remains a very good market, and Infosys will continue to grow and invest there.

subsections.label: Acquisitions and Joint Ventures
points: Infosys announced a joint venture with Versent in Australia, pending regulatory approvals.
Infosys is looking at other acquisitions with a good pipeline of opportunities.

subsections.label: Visa and Regulatory Challenges
points: Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.
The company has built local hubs and increased local hiring to mitigate visa-related risks.

subsections.label: Guidance Update
points: Revenue growth guidance for the financial year is 2% to 3% in constant currency terms.
Operating margin guidance remains at 20% to 22% for the full year.

guidance_ledger.statement: The new guidance is growth between 2% and 3% in constant currency terms for the full year.
Our operating margin guidance remains the same as in the past quarter at 20% to 22% for the full year.

risk_register.note: Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.

themes.label: AI Initiatives
themes.note: Infosys is scaling its AI initiatives with a large team of forward-deployed engineers.

themes.label: Large Deals
themes.note: Infosys announced a mega deal worth $1.6 billion after the close of the quarter.

themes.label: Revenue Growth Guidance
themes.note: Revenue growth guidance for the financial year is 2% to 3% in constant currency terms.

themes.label: Visa and Regulatory Challenges
themes.note: Infosys has taken steps to reduce dependence on work visas, especially H-1Bs in the U.S.

themes.label: Regional Performance
themes.note: Europe has done extremely well with good investments in different countries.

key_takeaways: Infosys reported strong Q2 FY26 performance with revenue growth and increased market share.
The company announced a mega deal worth $1.6 billion after the close of the quarter.
Revenue growth guidance for the financial year is 2% to 3% in constant currency terms.
Operating margin guidance remains at 20% to 22% for the full year.
Infosys is scaling its AI initiatives with a large team of forward-deployed engineers.
pressing_questions: Are you not seeing a meaningful recovery, especially when it comes to your Manufacturing, Retail, these kind of verticals?
Could you give us in percentage terms, how many of your employees that you were sending abroad were on H-1B visas?
What kind of contribution to revenue do you expect from the Versent acquisition?
In terms of the AI talk that is going on, could you tell us if you would be looking at anything of this sort this year in the next quarter or fourth quarter?
